title: Extract Favorite Letters from a Word

content.problemStatement:
Imagine you have a word entered by the user, but you only want to view a specific selection of its letters. In this task, you will first convert the word into a list of characters and then apply slicing from the start to extract a portion of it based on user input.

**Steps to Complete**


**Take User Input**: Accept a word as input from the user.
**Convert to List**: Use `list()` to convert the word into a list of characters.
**Take Input for Slicing**:

The end index (`end`) from the user (up to which characters should be extracted, exclusive).


**Apply Slicing from Start**: Use word_list`[:end]` to extract characters from the start up to end.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains a word (a non‑empty string of up to 100 printable characters, without spaces). The second line contains an integer end (0 ≤ end ≤ 100) – the exclusive index up to which characters are taken from the start of the word.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the Python‑style list representation of the selected characters, e.g. ['a', 'b', 'c']. If no characters are selected, output [] .

sampleTestCases.explanation: The word "python" sliced up to index 3 (exclusive) yields the characters p, y and t.
End index 0 selects no characters, so the result is an empty list.
Slicing up to the length of the word returns all its characters.

solution.approach: For each test case we read the word and the integer end. The word is converted to a list of its characters by simply iterating over the string. If end is larger than the word length we cap it to the length (the same behaviour as Python slicing). Then we output the first end characters in the format ['c1', 'c2', ...]. The algorithm is O(L) per test case where L is the length of the word, and uses O(L) auxiliary space only for building the output string.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * L) where L is the length of each word (≤ 100).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(L) for the temporary list of characters per test case.
